author: @agentbertram4769
text:
You'll love this one.
I have a mate who was the head of Health & Safety in this region.  When Beloved and I were self building our house, this guy came to help every weekend which was very decent of him.  It was sometimes a pain though, as he was overly attentive to H&S issues.  (However he continues to this day to be a good chum).
As our log walls progressed upwards, the day arrived when he asked if I had hard hats for everyone on site, (all three of us).  I had preempted him and produced them from a box with a flourish.  (I don't like hard hats as they make me sweat uncontrollably).
An hour later, he went outside to shove a flexible duct through the sub wall and into the solum, from underneath the veranda.  (We built the veranda early in order to give us a working platform to help with the walls).
The veranda proved difficult for him to work under, as at 1200mm above sub floor level he found that the hard hat kept catching on the structural veranda beams.  So, he took the hard hat off and left it atop the veranda floor.  Ten minutes later, he returned with a nasty cut in his scalp...  
As the build progressed, he proved to have no spacial awareness and kept banging his bonce on every available beam.  We say that there is more of his DNA in our home than anyone else's.  :O) 
In my very experienced opinion, Health & Safety has gone bonkers these days.  Public servants have made it their own fiefdoms and go over-the-top at every opportunity.  
Using a Stanley knife blade in this way Rob used to be called 'a skill' that you have developed, and long may it last!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: lol - awesome story mate - i ama firm believer in Darwin awards by the way lol

author: @donation1913
text:
These are the videos I believe help complete the perspective with the home brew generator maker.

The issue is that the physics of EMF is taught in such a way where it is difficult for the average person, me included, to grasp it in a manner which is applicable for their enthusiastic pursuits.
For instance, Motional EMF is almost always taught with the perspective that a coil is completely immersed in a magnetic flux field, and as it rotates or the flux increases or decreases, and so generates an EMF. The Home Brew individual will not be immersing their coils completely within a magnetic field and so the formulas as explained in those types of  physics demonstrations do not exactly apply to this different situation.

The first three videos are essential in our understanding of EMF creation with coils and magnetism. The fourth video will reinforce and explain in greater detail the math and relationships. The fourth video will also highlight my point about the perspective taught, which is not very helpful to the average individual building a generator at home. It's sort of like sitting at a poker table in a casino and asking the player next to you to teach you how to play.

Essential:

Measuring magnetic flux density for beginners
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=3nGc44rYXww

Magnetic flux and magnetic flux density for beginners
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=3NYg34_vy7k

7.1.3 Motional emf
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=u7Msmn8Fy-k

The following video assumes coils are always completely immersed in a magnetic field:

8.02x - Lect 17 - Motional EMF, Dynamos, Eddy Currents, Magnetic Breaking
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=MzAPu_p2wI4

Bonus video :-) which explains the is-ness of the universe:

Maxwell's Equations - Basic derivation
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=AWI70HXrbG0

The force and vector relationships of Electro-Magnetism is what physics has variously called Aether/Strings/God Particles. They are all trying to explain this one is-ness.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +Don Ation good guidance mate - thank you very much

author: @Allwestconstruction
text: Ha! Living on the wild side mate

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +Steve Allwest lol - right at the edge eh lol

replies.author: @Allwestconstruction
replies.text:
+Robert Murray-Smith Hi Robert, I was wondering if you could help me out on my hobby, I play around with making batteries (fun stuff) I am not sure if what I am making is normal or something worth pursuing to market, not much info out there to answer the question 
The cells are mostly copper and magnesium , and graphite and magnesium, I think the novelty of the cells I make is that it eats the whole magnesium down to nothing, rather than just eating the surface, I have had cells shorted through the meter on long term tests, that have run for over 60 hours, only using a 1/8" rod of magnesium 
I would appreciate any thoughts you may have on this matter 
Thanks

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +Steve Allwest Taking a product to market is a whole new ball game mate. I am not saying don't do it but be prepared for a lot of very frustrating work. The first thing you need to do is find your niche. Your batteries sound like primary batteries so you are going to have to find some unique USPs that shows how you battery is batter - in some aspect - than batteries currently on the market - this doesn't have to be in terms of energy storage - it can be cost, 'greeness' recyclability, ease of manufacture loads of things but if it doesn't have them it is a no starter as no one will manufacture for you and if you do toll manufacture no one will buy them unless youcan show how they are better in some aspect. So - step 1 - find the USPs - unique sales points

replies.author: @Allwestconstruction
replies.text:
+Robert Murray-Smith Yes, I believe that this is a primary battery, (you can charge it, but it only brings it back somewhat) although you can replace the anode and it is good for another 60 hours +
The problem is I do not know enough about batteries to know if this is unique, or where to find out, I have been researching and found nothing that compares with this, most of the info on batteries have a very short life span before needing charge again (maybe 120 to 200 miles on the Tesla)which if you drove at 60 miles an hours, that would mean you have just over 3 hours drive time before needing a recharge, which takes between 8 to 20 hours   

Although this is a primary battery, it would allow you to drive across the states with no battery charge needed, and when the battery life is done, you just replace the anode, (if you drove 60 miles an hour you would have a range of 3,600 miles) with this type of battery  

The anode is basically a solid fuel for the battery  

Your thoughts?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +Steve Allwest you are right mate - you are talking about a mechanically rechargable battery - which is a plan for some types of aluminium battery. Time is not the issue - power stored is. I can run a D cell for months if i only draw 1 millamp per hour out of it but it won't last five seconds if i draw 40 amps out of it. You need to get some idea of power - try running something and measuring the voltage and amps - like i id in the type b and type c perspective videos

author: @donation1913
text:
Glad your feeling better. Keep that PH slightly alkaline and you'll be right as rain.

OHH Yeah! Axial Flux baby.

Some questions toward design:
If this will be a wind generator, have you decided on the blade design?
Which blade you choose will effect coil design as it relates to RPM. My vote is for a Finnish designed Helix blade.
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=3Mlj1pZyDAE

When these generators get spinning, the faster they spin the more power they make, what is the amperage limit you design for so excess heat doesn't demagnetize your magnets, or worse melt something?
Can you build in Current limiters while voltage can continue to increase?
Is Maximum Amps for Power Transmission a useful metric when designing your coils?
Example: http://www.powerstream.com/Wire_Size.htm

Should you choose Wye, Delta, All series, or All parallel to wire up your coils?

Does the inductance of the coils matter and how does it affect output?

Can I use smaller AWG wire in my coils to reduce cost and size or will the net result always be 4 kilos of copper to generate n watts no matter which wire size you choose?

This is my Open Office Spreadsheet Coil Calculator:
Variables include Area from 5square" to 40square", 100 RPM to 2000 RPM, and 4 of the most likely wire sizes for generator designs.
https://www.sendspace.com/file/qed3pc
I played around within some of the columns, adjusting to see different results, so you'll have to fix those columns so the outputs are correct.

Cheers

author: @free100x
text:
Hint hint. You really need to make up and keep a liter of C60 (oven dried) olive oil around the shop and use about a half a teaspoon once a week to stay healthy. Crush the C60 globs up into an ash like powder and then add it to the olive oil and shake or stir daily.  Over days/weeks/months the oil will turn red as the C60 dissolves into the olive oil, and then it is ready to take. The C60 will enter deep into every cell of you body and protect you. It is graphene for the body LOL Keep the oil in a cool dark place and most of it in the freezer to keep it fresh.

Anti viral/bacteria/radiation/free radicals. It almost makes a person bullet proof against many harms.

author: @mgalyean
text: I had no real issues with your safety procedures really (to each his own) until you put your eyes, without any eye protection, in plane with the rotating disk  that was desperately trying to ballistically launch a razor blade at high speed.  It hurt just to watch and had me cringing! :D

replies.author: @free100x
replies.text:
+Marty G 

I have been around working on things for about a half of century or more and have never seen a ballistically launched razor blade LOL What you are fantasizing goes outside the reality of the materials and situation. The blade has virtually no real mass (weight) and poor aerodynamics, so any force 'throwing it' would dissipate fast, like trying to throw a paper airplane as hard as you can. The wheel was turning much slower than it looks like in the video, so it can't impart that much force to start with. Which direction the disk spins also determines which direction the blade would fly, if you lost control of the blade, but it is not big a deal, with the speeds and what he was working with.The only real danger is minor cuts or abrasions to the hands, but that happens a lot working on things and wearing gloves looses touch and consumes more  time and energy.

Fantasy can be very irrational and fear almost always is, as well, and comes out of fantasy normally. I rarely wear safety glasses (cheap yellow tinted sunglasses), when working on things. These safety people are paid to install fear in people who then pass it along to others like a virus of fear LOL

Real safety is found in reality and understanding the world you are working with. For beginners in life some glasses and gloves help, but they are still in danger because they don't know or understand a lot yet, but "accidents" will teach them LOL For the last 10,000 years very few have become blinded, by not wearing safety goggles out of billions without them. Being stupid can not only blind you but kill you, so that is safety number 1, and we all get stupid sometimes and it is best not to rely on safety equipment unless new, or if past experience says it is a real good idea.

Have a good one.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +Marty G that is really not something to worry about mate

replies.author: @mgalyean
replies.text: +free100x I once had to wear an eyepatch for a week because of a scratched cornea from a rubber band fired at me in a rubber band fight, I kid you not.  As for clarifying that weight is related to mass, I can't thank you enough.  Anyway, I think you missed the smiley in my original post (please don't miss the one in this one).  But that is a nice thesis you wrote in response.  Any decent professor would give you a good grade for the effort alone!  :^D

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +Marty G I like you mate - you're a good bloke with some sensible ideas - but even at the risk of upsetting you - you are preaching fear - even with a smiley face - life is risk - without risk there is nothing - I know you think you can mitigate risk and to a degree you can - it's really a question of should you. I think you are American? - Think of your own country - the great advancements you made the hectic rush to superpower status - all built on those people prepared to take a risk. And now? America is slipping into obscurity falling behind countries like China and India - mostly - I think - because you have become risk adverse. I sincerely hope this doesn't piss you off

replies.author: @mgalyean
replies.text: +Robert Murray-Smith,  you guys are cracking me up by dwelling on this so much. I'm not upset, and never was upset in this thread that started with a joke.  And for the record, Britain fell into obscurity first and America isn't there yet (damn the torpedoes! full speed ahead!).  :^D

author: @jerrywhidby5259
text: Can you flame polish the edges of this material?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +Jerry Whidby it's acrylic mate - i wouldn't flame polish it - but hey give it a go and let me know how it goes for you

replies.author: @TheBaconWizard
replies.text:
+Jerry Whidby  suspect you thought as I did that this is polycarb. 
But if you were thinking in terms of a heat-gun or torch I can tell you that it's not precise enough: It tends to go from just softening enough to bend it, to discoloured, expanding, and bubbling without a nice melty-bit in between. I'm guessing the companies that extrude the stuff have quite precise temperature controlled dies/rollers etc.

replies.author: @Robonza
replies.text: +Robert Murray-Smith Acrylic flame polishes beautifully  There is a knack to it though.
